questions.prompt: A new customer was onboarded three days ago. The detection queue is full of PUP autoruns and noisy signals. A peer suggests batch-closing them all as benign. What is wrong with this approach?
choices: Trap 1. The wave of look-alike detections always includes the one that was not. Batch-closure dismisses the real Low or Critical with the noise. The right path for suppression is the exclusion-request flow.

choices.label: Nothing. New customers always generate noise, so batch-closure is the expected workflow.
choices.rationale: Inverts the rule. New-customer noise is the highest-risk window for Trap 1.

choices.label: The peer should file a SIEM tuning request instead.
choices.rationale: Wrong surface. SIEM tuning is category 3 mandatory escalation, and EDR suppression goes through the exclusion-request flow.

choices.label: Reach out to the customer and ask which detections they are comfortable closing.
choices.rationale: Wrong process. The customer does not authorise dismissals.

questions.explanation: The first-week noise window is exactly where Trap 1 lives. Suppression through dismissive closures is the trap; suppression through the exclusion-request flow is the right path.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson describe second-guessing the SOC as a "trap" rather than a one-off mistake?
choices: The pattern compounds. The next disposition gets overridden too, and the relationship with the SOC degrades over time. Customer-specific context belongs as a clarifying reply, not a unilateral override.

choices.label: The SOC tracks per-tech override counts and reports them to your MSP.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing. The compounding is internal to the tech's habits and the senior's review.

choices.label: Huntress charges per override action.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the commercial model.

choices.label: Every override invalidates the original Incident Report.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the data model.

questions.explanation: The trap is the compounding pattern, not any single override. Customer-specific context goes through the clarifying-reply channel.

questions.prompt: You have just isolated a user's laptop on a High EDR incident. The user is unreachable: no answer on phone, no Teams response. You are tempted to move on to the next ticket. What is the right move?
choices: Try the documented backup contact (the customer's IT manager or executive assistant) within the same comms window. Leaving the user dark without a backup-channel attempt is Trap 3.

choices.label: Move on. The user will work it out when their machine does not connect.
choices.rationale: Trap 3 in real time. The user's experience of 'my machine just died, no one explained' lingers longer than the incident.

choices.label: Un-isolate the host until you can reach the user.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Reversing a correct containment action because comms is hard is the wrong trade-off.

choices.label: Wait an hour, then try the user again.
choices.rationale: Too slow. The comms window is minutes, not an hour.

questions.explanation: The isolation is correct. The comms responsibility is also non-negotiable. Backup contacts live within the same minutes-of-isolation window.

questions.prompt: 17:00. Three Lows closed in Huntress today, but two have PSA tickets still open. What do you do?

choices.label: Close all PSAs quickly with generic time entry and category.
choices.rationale: Trap 4 in real time. Generic time and category creates reporting drift.

choices: Spend the necessary minutes closing each PSA properly: time entry per actual time, specific category, notes.

choices.label: Leave them for tomorrow morning.
choices.rationale: Drift grows overnight. Tomorrow starts behind.

choices.label: Delete the PSA tickets since the Huntress side is closed.
choices.rationale: The PSA ticket is the canonical record. Deleting it loses the time entry, category, and audit trail entirely.

questions.explanation: End-of-shift discipline catches Trap 4 reliably. Both systems, every time, even when end-of-shift makes it tempting to skip.

questions.prompt: The lesson says all four traps share a common shape. What is it?
choices: Each is a small-friction shortcut that saves seconds for the tech and costs hours or relationships downstream.

choices.label: Each involves overriding the SOC's recommendation.
choices.rationale: Only Trap 2 directly involves overriding. Traps 1, 3, and 4 are workflow shortcuts.

choices.label: Each violates a Huntress licensing term.
choices.rationale: Licensing is not what the traps share.

choices.label: Each requires senior approval that the tech bypasses.
choices.rationale: Most of the traps are tech-level failures the senior catches in review, not bypassed approvals.

questions.explanation: Recognising the shortcut-shape makes all four visible as variants of the same underlying pattern.

questions.prompt: The four traps split into two pairs. Which is the right split?
choices: Recommendation-side: Trap 1 (suppressing alerts) and Trap 2 (second-guessing SOC). Workflow-side: Trap 3 (isolating without comms) and Trap 4 (closing Huntress but not PSA).

choices.label: Recommendation-side: Traps 3 and 4. Workflow-side: Traps 1 and 2.
choices.rationale: Inverts the mapping.

choices.label: Recommendation-side: Trap 1 only. Workflow-side: Traps 2, 3, and 4.
choices.rationale: Wrong split. Trap 2 is the most clearly recommendation-side trap.

choices.label: All four are workflow-side.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The lesson explicitly names the two-pair split.

questions.explanation: Traps 1 and 2 are about skipping reads and overriding analysis. Traps 3 and 4 are about skipping comms and admin.

questions.prompt: Match the traps to their characteristic detection surfaces.
choices: Trap 1 surfaces in the senior's closed-incident review. Trap 3 surfaces in next-morning customer friction. Trap 4 surfaces in the end-of-shift self-check.

choices.label: All four traps surface only in the LMS final assessment.
choices.rationale: The traps surface in live work signals; the assessment is calibration, not detection.

choices.label: Huntress automatically flags all four traps to the senior.
choices.rationale: Huntress does not auto-flag tech-side traps.

choices.label: Trap 4 surfaces in customer escalations; Trap 3 surfaces in PSA reporting.
choices.rationale: Inverts the mapping. Trap 4 is PSA drift; Trap 3 is customer friction.

questions.explanation: Each trap has its own characteristic detection surface. The end-of-shift self-check catches Trap 4 reliably.

questions.prompt: The four traps are advanced mistakes that only senior techs make, not new-tech mistakes.
questions.explanation: The opposite. These are the most common new-tech failures specifically because the shortcuts feel reasonable in the moment to someone new.

questions.prompt: You realise you did not call the user post-isolation yesterday. The customer's IT manager called this morning frustrated about a "surprise lockout." What is the right next move?

choices.label: Move on. The incident is closed.
choices.rationale: The customer-relationship piece is part of close-out discipline. Ignoring it lets Trap 3 stand uncorrected.

choices: Add a note to the PSA ticket reflecting what happened, flag the situation to your senior tomorrow. The customer-relationship recovery is the next step after the senior weighs in.

choices.label: Apologise to the IT manager by email right now.
choices.rationale: Premature. Customer-relationship recovery has tone and possibly account-manager involvement. The senior should weigh in first.

choices.label: Delete the offending PSA note about the lockout so the audit trail looks cleaner.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Tampering with the audit trail compounds Trap 3 with a separate integrity issue.

questions.explanation: Name the trap, document what happened, get the senior in the loop. Do not reach out to the customer before the senior weighs in on tone.

questions.prompt: Avoiding the four traps means working significantly slower on every ticket.
questions.explanation: Avoiding the traps takes seconds, not minutes. The shortcut savings are illusory. The downstream cost of each trap is hours or relationships.
